details.body:
finding_summary: The branch never implements Part B of the bead: `result` is still tracked in `HEAD`, so `git ls-files -i -c --exclude-standard` still reports it and future checkpoint branches will continue to carry an unrelated `result` diff until the symlink is actually untracked.
severity: high
affected_files:
- result:1
remediation_hint: Run `git rm --cached -- result` on this branch and commit that deletion without deleting the working-tree symlink.

Part B of the bead (untracking `result`) was not performed. `result` is still present in the git index on this branch (`git ls-files result` returns it; `git ls-files -i -c --exclude-standard` on HEAD lists `result`). The only commit on the branch (7518cc15) actually updates the `result` symlink target rather than removing it from the index.

The bead explicitly states: "This has two parts. Both must land in this PR." It also lists as acceptance criteria:
- `result` is no longer tracked on `master` (post-merge).
- `git ls-files -i -c --exclude-standard` on a fresh clone of master prints nothing.

Neither is currently met. Additionally, shipping Part A without Part B has a concrete downstream effect: the next post-merge ralph-burning checkpoint whose parent still tracks `result` will produce a tree that omits `result`, so the checkpoint commit diff shows `result` as a spurious **deletion** rather than the old symlink-target update — the review noise is reshaped rather than eliminated.

Remediation (as specified by the bead): run `git rm --cached -- result` from the repo root and commit the removal on this branch. Do not delete the physical symlink. No other files need untracking — `git ls-files -i -c --exclude-standard` currently returns only `result`.

Affected files: `result` (index entry), repo root.

finding_summary: Paths returned by `git ls-files -z ...` are byte strings, but the new ignored-path filtering lossy-decodes them to `String` before feeding them back to `git update-index --force-remove`. That breaks tracked gitignored and assume-unchanged files whose names are not valid UTF-8: the temporary checkpoint index keeps the original raw path, so the checkpoint still leaks that file. I reproduced this with a temp repo containing `bad\xff.txt`, where removing the lossy-decoded name exits 0 but the raw path remains in the index.
affected_files: `src/adapters/worktree.rs:134-138`, `src/adapters/worktree.rs:148-157`, `src/adapters/worktree.rs:170-174`, `tests/unit/checkpoint_test.rs:289-358`
remediation_hint: Preserve raw path bytes / `OsString` values through the round-trip and pass them back to Git without UTF-8 conversion, for example by building command args from `OsStrExt::from_bytes` or by using a NUL-delimited stdin path channel.

src/adapters/worktree.rs:155-185 force-removes every `assume-unchanged` path from the checkpoint index, and tests/unit/checkpoint_test.rs:383-418 codifies that behavior. These files are still tracked, so removing them from the tree turns them into deletions instead of preserving the parent-tree version. In a simple git repro, the resulting checkpoint cannot be restored: `git reset --hard <checkpoint>` fails with `Entry 'assume.txt' not uptodate. Cannot merge.` once the checkpoint commit has dropped the file. This breaks the safety contract of checkpoints for any repo that uses `assume-unchanged` locally.

src/adapters/worktree.rs:137-152 queries ignored paths with `git ls-files -i -c --exclude-standard`, then src/adapters/worktree.rs:173-185 removes every match from the checkpoint tree. `--exclude-standard` also honors `.git/info/exclude` and the developer's global `core.excludesFile`, so checkpoint contents now depend on user-local git configuration rather than repository state. A local global-ignore rule matching a tracked file is enough to make that file disappear from checkpoint commits entirely. That is nondeterministic and can silently corrupt checkpoints on one machine but not another.

Remove the stray `prompt-t7j.md` file from the repo root before merging. It was added at the root by the first checkpoint commit (7518cc15) via `git add -A -- .`, is byte-identical to `.ralph-burning/projects/t7j-1/prompt.md`, and is not covered by `.gitignore`. It is therefore not excluded by the Part A fix and would land on master if this branch is merged, polluting the top-level tree with a transient scratchpad duplicate of the bead prompt. Recommended remediation: `git rm prompt-t7j.md && git commit -m "chore: remove stray bead prompt scratchpad from repo root"`. This is directly adjacent to the bead's stated acceptance goal of keeping master clean.

`src/adapters/worktree.rs:142-152` narrows tracked ignored-path detection to `git ls-files -i -c --exclude-per-directory=.gitignore`, and `build_checkpoint_tree` only removes the paths returned by that scan (`src/adapters/worktree.rs:269-274`). That means tracked files ignored via other standard Git ignore sources, notably `.git/info/exclude` and `core.excludesFile`, still survive into checkpoint commits. The new tests at `tests/unit/checkpoint_test.rs:437-533` explicitly lock in that narrower behavior. The bead requires the standard-ignore behavior (`--exclude-standard` or equivalent), so this leaves `rb: checkpoint` incomplete and still able to leak unrelated locally ignored tracked files into diffs.

finding_summary: `build_checkpoint_tree` deletes tracked-but-gitignored files from checkpoint commits instead of preserving their parent blob. `src/adapters/worktree.rs:263-268` unconditionally `update-index --force-remove`s every tracked ignored path after `read-tree` loaded the parent tree, so a checkpoint/reset turns any still-tracked ignored file into a real deletion. The new assertions in `tests/unit/checkpoint_test.rs:352-385` and `tests/unit/checkpoint_test.rs:437-525` lock in that destructive behavior.
affected_files:
- src/adapters/worktree.rs:263-268
- tests/unit/checkpoint_test.rs:352-385
- tests/unit/checkpoint_test.rs:437-525
remediation_hint: Keep tracked gitignored paths at their parent/index entry in the temporary index, the same way assume-unchanged paths are restored, and compute the ignored set from the pre-staging tracked state so ignored edits/deletions do not leak without deleting the tracked file from the checkpoint tree.

finding_summary: `build_checkpoint_tree` restores ignored entries back into the temporary checkpoint index instead of dropping them, so checkpoint commits can resurrect files that were already intentionally untracked.
affected_files:
- `src/adapters/worktree.rs:265-283`
- `tests/unit/checkpoint_test.rs:352-438`
remediation_hint: After `git add -A` in the temp index, enumerate ignored / assume-unchanged paths in that temp index and remove them with `git update-index --force-remove -- ...`; then update the regression to assert those paths are absent from the checkpoint tree instead of preserving the parent blob.

This is not just a spec mismatch. In the `rm --cached` case covered by `worktree_adapter_preserves_parent_version_for_tracked_gitignored_paths_even_if_real_index_untracks_them`, the checkpoint commit reintroduces `ignored.txt` from the parent tree. A later `reset_to_checkpoint` will therefore re-track a file the user explicitly removed from the index, which is the opposite of the bead's goal for `result`-style paths.

finding_summary: [src/adapters/worktree.rs:161-172] `assume_unchanged_paths` misparses `git ls-files -z -s -v` output by splitting on every tab and taking `.nth(1)`. Git only uses the first tab as the metadata/path separator, so a legal assume-unchanged filename like `tab\tfile.txt` is truncated to `tab`. `remove_paths_from_index` then removes nothing, and the checkpoint tree still retains the assume-unchanged file. That means the new filtering logic is still incorrect for valid Git paths containing tabs. affected_files: src/adapters/worktree.rs, tests/unit/checkpoint_test.rs. remediation_hint: split each record only once at the first tab (`splitn(2, ...)` / equivalent byte-slice logic) and add a regression next to `worktree_adapter_omits_assume_unchanged_paths_from_checkpoint_commits` for an assume-unchanged path containing a tab.

finding_summary: `build_checkpoint_tree` now removes tracked ignored/assume-unchanged paths from the checkpoint tree entirely, so the checkpoint commit records them as deletions instead of freezing their previously tracked contents.
affected_files:
- src/adapters/worktree.rs:218-223
- src/adapters/worktree.rs:520-548
- src/adapters/worktree.rs:600-601
- tests/unit/checkpoint_test.rs:347-660
remediation_hint: After `git add -A`, restore ignored/assume-unchanged paths to their current real-index/parent-tree entry instead of `update-index --force-remove` whenever the path is still tracked there; only remove paths that are already absent from the real index (for example after an explicit `git rm --cached`).

src/adapters/worktree.rs:137-152 and 280-289 discover tracked gitignored paths only from the temporary index after git add -A has already rewritten it. If a tracked-but-gitignored path is deleted or renamed in the worktree, add -A removes it from that index first, so tracked_gitignored_paths returns nothing and the checkpoint commit records an unrelated deletion. The new tests only cover modified ignored files, not this cleanup/remove case. Derive the candidate ignored set from the real index before staging, or otherwise merge the pre-add tracked ignored paths into the filter set so deletions are suppressed too.
